romania.summary: World Vision România: Avem nevoie de o strategie privind folosirea AI în școală și educație media Ziua Siguranței pe Internet,... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România: 99% dintre adolescenți folosesc AI, 80.96% au întâlnit conținut fals creat cu AI, iar 37.25% învață despre folosirea sigură a AI din online și social media apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: UK parents told to limit under-5s screen time
medical.summary: The UK government on Friday published its first national guidance on screen time for children, advising parents to allow no more than an hour a day for under-fives.

medical.title: Can marijuana cause lung cancer?
medical.summary: As more states legalize marijuana, many people might be wondering if that means it is safe to use. While smoking tobacco is a known and well-studied cause of lung cancer, linking marijuana to cancer is more complicated, says Brooks Udelsman, MD, a thoracic surgeon with USC Surgery, part of Keck Medicine of USC.

medical.title: Study finds a U-shaped link between ovarian cancer diagnosis time and survival
medical.summary: Ovarian cancer is hard to diagnose early. Its symptoms, like bloating and abdominal pain, are vague and similar to other more common conditions. Early diagnosis improves outcomes for many cancers. However, prior research suggests that faster diagnosis does not improve ovarian cancer survival, discouraging investments in better diagnostic tools. One possible explanation that could explain these prior findings is the "wait time paradox": the sickest patients are easier to diagnose quickly but also have poorer outcomes.

medical.title: Strong patient diversity in biobanks reveals new genetic links to disease risk and treatment response
medical.summary: A new study by UCLA Health published in Cell presents a major advancement in the future of personalized medicine by pinpointing new connections between people's genes, disease risk and medicine response by using a clinically well-characterized and diverse population-represented biobank.

medical.title: Why COVID and flu hit older lungs harder: Aging tissue may bring on immune dysregulation
medical.summary: Older adults are much more likely to become seriously ill from flu or COVID because aging lung cells can drive excessive immune responses, according to a new study led by researchers at UC San Francisco. The findings enhance the understanding of the inflammation that accompanies aging, explaining how an otherwise minor cough can sometimes send an elderly person to the hospital.

medical.title: Q&A: How much protein is enough?
medical.summary: Protein-maxxing—the social media-fueled trend of maximizing dietary protein at every opportunity—is showing no sign of slowing. That's in part because the federal government has weighed in with nutrition guidelines that emphasize the consumption of meat and dairy and an increase in the recommended dietary allowance of protein by 50% to 100% over the previous RDA.

medical.title: What to know about shingles, a painful infection that vaccination can prevent
medical.summary: NBA star Tyrese Haliburton was recently diagnosed with shingles. The news drew attention to an illness that many people rarely talk about but is far more common than many realize.

medical.title: Is dark chocolate healthier than milk chocolate? Dietitians explain
medical.summary: Easter chocolate is all over supermarket shelves. Some people reach straight for milk chocolate eggs while others pause at the darker varieties, assuming they're healthier.

medical.title: AI learns to make sense of childhood cancer survivors' health care needs
medical.summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) could help physicians determine if survivors of childhood cancer need extra support—and the more information included in AI prompting, the better its performance. This finding, published in Communications Medicine by scientists from St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, may guide future integration of AI into clinical workflows.

medical.title: IBS diets don't work for everyone: New research shows why—and it's not just about the food
medical.summary: If you've ever tried a diet to fix gut symptoms, you'll know it can be hit or miss. One person swears it changed their life. Another follows it carefully and feels no better.

medical.title: 10 ways that future urban living will be greener
medical.summary: From carbon-negative homes to people-powered street lights, new innovations are transforming urban living The post 10 ways that future urban living will be greener appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: From simulation to strategy: Climate modeling motivates action at the top
science.summary: New research by MIT Sloan School of Management finds that global leaders who participate in facilitated engagements using an interactive climate policy simulator, En-ROADS, demonstrated a stronger understanding of climate solutions, felt more personally connected to the issue, and were more likely to take climate-related action or advocate for change in their governments, businesses, organizations, or communities. Most reported that the interactive aspects of simulations enhanced learning compared to conventional, more passive approaches.

science.title: Study in search of a tropical spring is first to show some birds flip their breeding season in response to climate
science.summary: In 2014, Felicity Newell joined the Florida Museum of Natural History as a doctoral student, then promptly left the country in search of a tropical spring. It's a concept she started thinking about while doing biological surveys in Honduras. There, a colleague told her about the work of Alexander Skutch, a renowned ornithologist who spent 20 years studying the breeding habits of birds in Costa Rica. Based on this work, he became "convinced that the birds … have a definite nesting season, and its beginning coincides with the return of spring."

science.title: Satellite Spots a Spawn
science.summary: The activity of herring around Vancouver Island in British Columbia brightened coastal waters enough to be detectable from space.

science.title: From engineered fungal molecules to drug leads, chem-bio hybrid synthesis enables antiparasitic drug discovery
science.summary: Amebiasis is a parasitic disease caused by the microscopic protozoan Entamoeba histolytica. Infection occurs through the ingestion of cysts from contaminated water or food. Worldwide, approximately 50 million symptomatic cases are estimated annually, mainly in tropical and subtropical regions.

science.title: Scientists may be overestimating amounts of microplastics in the environment, and the culprit is lab gloves
science.summary: It seems like every day a new study finds tiny plastic particles called microplastics where they should not be: in our bodies and our food, water and air.

science.title: Study finds 60% of Australia's top-use pesticides are banned in the EU
science.summary: Analysis of Australia's highest-volume pesticide use has revealed that the majority of products widely used in Australian agriculture are banned in other parts of the world, according to a new study. Researchers from Griffith's School of Environment and Science analyzed 45 pesticide products used in volumes of more than 100 metric tons per year, identified through the Australian Government's now archived Agricultural Chemical Usage Database.

science.title: Fieldoscopy reveals femtosecond optical switching in 15 nm indium tin oxide nanocrystals
science.summary: Just as an antenna interacts with radio waves, light interacts with metallic nanostructures. Therefore, understanding how a structure influences field oscillations provides valuable insights into the structure's physical properties. An international research team, including scientists from the Max Planck Institute for the Science of Light (MPL), is investigating the changes in field oscillations that occur when light interacts with indium tin oxide (ITO) nanocrystals. This will deepen our understanding of how the interaction between light and these nanocrystals depends on time.

science.title: How labor movements may help rebalance power in the technology sector
science.summary: Wide swaths of American workers are more meaningfully involved than in the past in organizing around and pushing back against artificial intelligence (AI) and data-driven technologies in the workplace, a Rutgers study has found. Rutgers researchers examined the 2023 contract for the labor union Screen Actors Guild-American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (SAG-AFTRA). Their research highlights strategies for workers to use to push back through forming workers collectives around technology, developing more people-centered policies and forging solidarities with transnational labor.

science.title: NASA’s Hubble accidentally caught a comet breaking apart in real time
science.summary: In an incredibly lucky cosmic accident, NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope captured a comet breaking apart in real time—something astronomers have long tried and failed to observe. The comet, C/2025 K1 (ATLAS), wasn’t even the original target, but when researchers pivoted to it, they unknowingly caught it mid-disintegration into multiple pieces.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
mix_items.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

mix_items.title: Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil
mix_items.summary: Află povestea lui Ionuț și cum sprijinul educațional și emoțional oferit de Fundația Inocenți ajută copiii vulnerabili să își descopere încrederea în sine. The post Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: 36 de tineri artiști vizuali și muzicieni, susținuți de către Fundația Regală Margareta a României, se prezintă publicului pe 2 martie, la Elite Art Gallery, București Fundația Regală Margareta a României anunță Identități, evenimentul care marchează debutul public al generației 2026 a programului național... mix_items.summary: Pentru Alina Măciucă, educația nu se oprește la ușa clasei și nici la granițele unei țări. Profesor pentru învățământ preșcolar... mix_items.title: UK parents told to limit under-5s screen time
mix_items.summary: The UK government on Friday published its first national guidance on screen time for children, advising parents to allow no more than an hour a day for under-fives.

mix_items.title: Beneficiile ieșirilor culturale în dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Descoperă beneficiile ieșirilor culturale pentru copii și modul în care muzeele, teatrul și excursiile contribuie la dezvoltarea lor emoțională și educațională. The post Beneficiile ieșirilor culturale în dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Can marijuana cause lung cancer?
mix_items.summary: As more states legalize marijuana, many people might be wondering if that means it is safe to use. While smoking tobacco is a known and well-studied cause of lung cancer, linking marijuana to cancer is more complicated, says Brooks Udelsman, MD, a thoracic surgeon with USC Surgery, part of Keck Medicine of USC.

mix_items.summary: Joi, 12 februarie, de la ora 18:00, la Librăria Libris (Str. Șt. O. Iosif) din Brașov, Rodica Bretin lansează volumul „Pisica de la ora cinci”, apărut în colecția Corint Fiction. … Read More The post Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Sondaj World Vision România: 99% dintre adolescenți folosesc AI, 80.96% au întâlnit conținut fals creat cu AI, iar 37.25% învață despre folosirea sigură a AI din online și social media
mix_items.summary: World Vision România: Avem nevoie de o strategie privind folosirea AI în școală și educație media Ziua Siguranței pe Internet,... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România: 99% dintre adolescenți folosesc AI, 80.96% au întâlnit conținut fals creat cu AI, iar 37.25% învață despre folosirea sigură a AI din online și social media apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Study finds a U-shaped link between ovarian cancer diagnosis time and survival
mix_items.summary: Ovarian cancer is hard to diagnose early. Its symptoms, like bloating and abdominal pain, are vague and similar to other more common conditions. Early diagnosis improves outcomes for many cancers. However, prior research suggests that faster diagnosis does not improve ovarian cancer survival, discouraging investments in better diagnostic tools. One possible explanation that could explain these prior findings is the "wait time paradox": the sickest patients are easier to diagnose quickly but also have poorer outcomes.

mix_items.title: Strong patient diversity in biobanks reveals new genetic links to disease risk and treatment response
mix_items.summary: A new study by UCLA Health published in Cell presents a major advancement in the future of personalized medicine by pinpointing new connections between people's genes, disease risk and medicine response by using a clinically well-characterized and diverse population-represented biobank.

mix_items.title: Why COVID and flu hit older lungs harder: Aging tissue may bring on immune dysregulation
mix_items.summary: Older adults are much more likely to become seriously ill from flu or COVID because aging lung cells can drive excessive immune responses, according to a new study led by researchers at UC San Francisco. The findings enhance the understanding of the inflammation that accompanies aging, explaining how an otherwise minor cough can sometimes send an elderly person to the hospital.

mix_items.title: Q&A: How much protein is enough?
mix_items.summary: Protein-maxxing—the social media-fueled trend of maximizing dietary protein at every opportunity—is showing no sign of slowing. That's in part because the federal government has weighed in with nutrition guidelines that emphasize the consumption of meat and dairy and an increase in the recommended dietary allowance of protein by 50% to 100% over the previous RDA.

mix_items.title: What to know about shingles, a painful infection that vaccination can prevent
mix_items.summary: NBA star Tyrese Haliburton was recently diagnosed with shingles. The news drew attention to an illness that many people rarely talk about but is far more common than many realize.

mix_items.title: Is dark chocolate healthier than milk chocolate? Dietitians explain
mix_items.summary: Easter chocolate is all over supermarket shelves. Some people reach straight for milk chocolate eggs while others pause at the darker varieties, assuming they're healthier.

mix_items.title: AI learns to make sense of childhood cancer survivors' health care needs
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) could help physicians determine if survivors of childhood cancer need extra support—and the more information included in AI prompting, the better its performance. This finding, published in Communications Medicine by scientists from St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, may guide future integration of AI into clinical workflows.

mix_items.title: IBS diets don't work for everyone: New research shows why—and it's not just about the food
mix_items.summary: If you've ever tried a diet to fix gut symptoms, you'll know it can be hit or miss. One person swears it changed their life. Another follows it carefully and feels no better.

mix_items.title: From simulation to strategy: Climate modeling motivates action at the top
mix_items.summary: New research by MIT Sloan School of Management finds that global leaders who participate in facilitated engagements using an interactive climate policy simulator, En-ROADS, demonstrated a stronger understanding of climate solutions, felt more personally connected to the issue, and were more likely to take climate-related action or advocate for change in their governments, businesses, organizations, or communities. Most reported that the interactive aspects of simulations enhanced learning compared to conventional, more passive approaches.

mix_items.title: Study in search of a tropical spring is first to show some birds flip their breeding season in response to climate
mix_items.summary: In 2014, Felicity Newell joined the Florida Museum of Natural History as a doctoral student, then promptly left the country in search of a tropical spring. It's a concept she started thinking about while doing biological surveys in Honduras. There, a colleague told her about the work of Alexander Skutch, a renowned ornithologist who spent 20 years studying the breeding habits of birds in Costa Rica. Based on this work, he became "convinced that the birds … have a definite nesting season, and its beginning coincides with the return of spring."

mix_items.title: Satellite Spots a Spawn
mix_items.summary: The activity of herring around Vancouver Island in British Columbia brightened coastal waters enough to be detectable from space.

mix_items.title: From engineered fungal molecules to drug leads, chem-bio hybrid synthesis enables antiparasitic drug discovery
mix_items.summary: Amebiasis is a parasitic disease caused by the microscopic protozoan Entamoeba histolytica. Infection occurs through the ingestion of cysts from contaminated water or food. Worldwide, approximately 50 million symptomatic cases are estimated annually, mainly in tropical and subtropical regions.

mix_items.title: Scientists may be overestimating amounts of microplastics in the environment, and the culprit is lab gloves
mix_items.summary: It seems like every day a new study finds tiny plastic particles called microplastics where they should not be: in our bodies and our food, water and air.

mix_items.title: Study finds 60% of Australia's top-use pesticides are banned in the EU
mix_items.summary: Analysis of Australia's highest-volume pesticide use has revealed that the majority of products widely used in Australian agriculture are banned in other parts of the world, according to a new study. Researchers from Griffith's School of Environment and Science analyzed 45 pesticide products used in volumes of more than 100 metric tons per year, identified through the Australian Government's now archived Agricultural Chemical Usage Database.

mix_items.title: Fieldoscopy reveals femtosecond optical switching in 15 nm indium tin oxide nanocrystals
mix_items.summary: Just as an antenna interacts with radio waves, light interacts with metallic nanostructures. Therefore, understanding how a structure influences field oscillations provides valuable insights into the structure's physical properties. An international research team, including scientists from the Max Planck Institute for the Science of Light (MPL), is investigating the changes in field oscillations that occur when light interacts with indium tin oxide (ITO) nanocrystals. This will deepen our understanding of how the interaction between light and these nanocrystals depends on time.

mix_items.title: How labor movements may help rebalance power in the technology sector
mix_items.summary: Wide swaths of American workers are more meaningfully involved than in the past in organizing around and pushing back against artificial intelligence (AI) and data-driven technologies in the workplace, a Rutgers study has found. Rutgers researchers examined the 2023 contract for the labor union Screen Actors Guild-American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (SAG-AFTRA). Their research highlights strategies for workers to use to push back through forming workers collectives around technology, developing more people-centered policies and forging solidarities with transnational labor.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Hubble accidentally caught a comet breaking apart in real time
mix_items.summary: In an incredibly lucky cosmic accident, NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope captured a comet breaking apart in real time—something astronomers have long tried and failed to observe. The comet, C/2025 K1 (ATLAS), wasn’t even the original target, but when researchers pivoted to it, they unknowingly caught it mid-disintegration into multiple pieces.

mix_items.title: 10 ways that future urban living will be greener
mix_items.summary: From carbon-negative homes to people-powered street lights, new innovations are transforming urban living The post 10 ways that future urban living will be greener appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am luat sticla de apa ca sa fiu adult responsabil. Am umplut-o cu suc.

satire_ro.title: Top 7 red flags când te întâlnești cu o gagică
